focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

The damp got through his trousers before anything else did. Kneeling by the plinth, Owen felt the old pressure build behind his molars until he either got the lines right or went back upstairs with a headache that made parchment shine. Above him the rest of Hogwarts still interrupted him like a school: bells, essay deadlines, Amycus roaring in a corridor, somebody at breakfast asking whether he meant to eat that bit of toast. By April the East Undercroft felt less like a discovery than a job he had not meant to take and could no longer leave half done. Martha had stopped speaking to him except when a doorway forced the issue. Prue still spoke, but not with any waste in it. Down here the stone did not ask what kind of person that made him. It only waited to see whether he had understood.

He had been wrong about the lower tables for nearly three weeks.

The mistake annoyed him in a practical way that was worse than embarrassment. He had copied the pages twice into the ash-linen notebook, once in his own cramped hand and once with the signs separated into columns the way Prue had suggested, and still he had kept treating the marked block beneath Hogwarts as if it were a repair rubric with half its instructions lost. The block was whole; repetition had hidden its purpose. The same cluster sat beneath all four anchors, shifted one place each time, so it governed relation rather than any local repair: held, thinned, under weight, in live water.

It described the condition under which one site answered to another. Spellwork sat further up the chain. This was site logic.

He put the notebook flat on the anchor stone and checked the copied marks again, rubbing the heel of his hand over the place where the page had softened from damp. The Hogwarts column did not en...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...once.

"This isn't a punishment," he said. "I just don't have room for doubt."

Then he left too.

Owen stood alone in the disused classroom with the torn-thread dust from the sheets still drifting in the late light. On the desk one peppermint had rolled out of the jar and come to rest against a crack in the wood. He stared at it for a second as if he had been assigned the task of accounting for sweets and had failed that as well.

When he finally went downstairs to the Hufflepuff common room, the round room was full in the ordinary way: homework, wet shoes by the fire, somebody arguing over a Transfiguration essay, bread smell coming faintly through the stones from the kitchens. The scene should have been a relief. Instead it felt arranged.

At the long central table two third-years were bent over a hand-drawn map of corridor turns. One of them, a boy with a patched sleeve and a habit of chewing the inside of his cheek, saw Owen come in and put his hand over the paper at once. He did it efficiently, not guiltily.

The other boy folded the map and slid it under his Potions text.

Neither of them said anything.

Owen crossed the room to his usual chair and set the satchel down between his shoes. The manuscript was still there. The notebook was still there. So was everything Snape had not taken, everything Neville would not now let near his own work, everything large and hidden and structurally wrong.

Around him the common room kept being warm, round, and Hufflepuff in the old stubborn way. Somebody laughed by the hearth. Somebody else asked for the butter. No one asked him to pass it.

By curfew Martha was nowhere in the common room. The folded map of corridor turns under the Potions text stayed in his head with the stupid persistence of something already half decided.
